grammar: "Hur mår du?" is the everyday informal greeting. Use "Hur står det till?" for more formal situations.
"God morgon" is used until about noon. After that, switch to "God dag" or just "Hej".
"God kväll" is typically used from around 6 PM onwards.
"Hej då" is the standard goodbye. "Vi ses" (see you) is more casual among friends.
"Jag heter" literally means "I am called." Swedes rarely use "Mitt namn är" in everyday speech.
"Heta" in present tense: heter. Used exclusively for names — never for temperature.
This is the standard phrase when meeting someone for the first time.
"Hur är det?" is slightly more casual than "Hur mår du?" — used between acquaintances.
"Mår" comes from "må" — used only for wellbeing. You can't use it for physical health.
"Varifrån" combines "var" (where) and "ifrån" (from).
"Komma från" = come from. "Komma" is irregular: kommer, kom, kommit.
Use "i" for cities and countries: "Jag bor i Göteborg," "Jag bor i Sverige."
Question word + verb + subject. The verb must always be in second position.
Nationality adjectives are not capitalized in Swedish: "svensk," "finsk," "norsk."
"Prata" is colloquial, "tala" is more formal. Both mean "to speak."
"Tack" alone is fine. "Tack så mycket" adds emphasis and warmth.
"Varsågod" also means "here you go" when handing something to someone.
"Ursäkta" is for getting attention. "Förlåt" is a deeper apology.
"Tyvärr" softens a refusal. Swedes often avoid blunt "nej" and add softeners.
Swedes say "jobba med" rather than "jobba som" — the standard way to ask about occupation.
Swedish drops the article before professions: "Jag är lärare," not "Jag är en lärare."

target: Det var trevligt att prata med dig.
english: It was nice talking to you.

grammar: "Vi ses" literally means "we see each other" — a casual, friendly goodbye.
"God natt" is only used when someone is going to sleep, unlike English evening farewells.
"Välkommen" for one person, "välkomna" for a group.
"Förstå" is irregular: förstår, förstod, förstått. Very high-frequency verb.
"Betyda" = mean. "Det betyder" = it means. Essential for language learners.

target: Hur säger man det på svenska?
english: How do you say that in Swedish?
grammar: "Man" is the impersonal pronoun, like English "one" or generic "you."
"Lära sig" is reflexive — you literally "teach yourself." Don't forget "mig."
"Ny" becomes "nytt" for ett-words and "nya" for plural or definite forms.

target: Det är mitt första besök i Sverige.
english: It is my first visit to Sweden.

grammar: "Semester" means vacation — a false friend with the English academic term.
"För" = for (purpose). Different from "för att" (because) and "för att" (in order to).
"Vad" + adjective is an exclamation pattern: "Vad fint!" "Vad roligt!"
Fika is a cornerstone of Swedish culture — a coffee break with pastry. It's both a noun and a verb.
"Låta" means both "to sound" and "to let/allow" depending on context.
"Hålla med" means to agree. Literally "hold with."
Cardinal numbers don't change form: "två barn," "två katter," "två hus."
"Gift" means both "married" and "poison" in Swedish — context makes it clear.
Many relationship words are English loans in Swedish: singel, dejt (date), flörta (flirt).
"Sambo" is a legally recognized status in Sweden, distinct from married.

target: Ska vi gå ut och äta?
english: Shall we go out to eat?

grammar: "Bjuda" means to treat/invite. "Jag bjuder" means you're paying.
When toasting, make eye contact with each person — an important Swedish custom.

english: What do you do in your free time?
grammar: "Fritid" = free time. A compound noun: "fri" (free) + "tid" (time).
"Gilla" is casual for "like." "Tycka om" is slightly more formal.
"Tycka om" = like. Don't confuse with "tycka" alone which means "think" (opinion).
"Trivas" conveys feeling at home and content — a uniquely Swedish concept.
"Mysig" captures the Swedish love of coziness — candles, warmth, good company.

target: Jag heter Anna och jag kommer från Malmö.
english: My name is Anna and I come from Malmö.
grammar: You can connect clauses with "och" (and) without changing word order.
"Vad" + adjective is a common exclamation pattern unique to Swedish.

target: Vi bor i en lägenhet i centrala Göteborg.
english: We live in an apartment in central Gothenburg.

grammar: "Min" = my (en-words). "Mitt" = my (ett-words). "Mina" = my (plural).

target: Jag har en bror och en syster.
english: I have a brother and a sister.

grammar: "Mina" is the plural possessive. Uppsala is home to Scandinavia's oldest university.
"Plugga" is everyday for studying. "Studera" is slightly more formal.

target: Hur länge har du bott här?
english: How long have you lived here?

target: Har du varit i Sverige förut?
english: Have you been to Sweden before?
grammar: "Förut" = before/previously. Placed at the end of the sentence.
"Jätte-" is a prefix meaning "very/really" — jättebra, jättefin, jättegod.

english: Do you want to come along?
grammar: "Hänga med" is casual for joining. More formal: "Vill du följa med?"
"Gärna" means gladly — a very Swedish way to accept an invitation.
"Ses" = see each other. Reciprocal s-verb, used for planning meetings.

target: Hej, jag heter Sven och det här är min fru.
english: Hi, my name is Sven and this is my wife.
We are here over the weekend.
grammar: "Över" = over/during: "över helgen" = over the weekend.

target: Vad fint väder det är idag!
english: What nice weather it is today!
grammar: Exclamation pattern: "Vad" + adjective + noun + "det är"!

target: Jag vill lära mig mer svenska.
english: I want to learn more Swedish.

grammar: "Vi hörs" literally means "we hear each other" — used like "talk to you later."
"Ha det bra" is one of the most common farewells, meaning "have it good."

english: We'll see each other again soon.
grammar: "Ses" is a reciprocal s-verb meaning "see each other" — reflexive by nature.
"Lycka till" = good luck! "Lycka" = luck/happiness. "Till" adds direction/purpose.
"Grattis" is informal. "Gratulerar" is the full formal version.

target: God jul och gott nytt år!
english: Merry Christmas and happy new year!

grammar: Midsommar is one of Sweden's biggest holidays — dancing, flowers, and herring around the solstice.
"Helg" can mean both "weekend" and "holiday." Context determines which.

target: Det här är min vän Lisa.

english: We have known each other since university.

grammar: "Hur har du det?" is slightly more intimate than "Hur mår du?" — asks about life situation.
"Det går bra" works as both "I'm fine" and "that works/is okay."

target: Jag är glad att träffa dig.
english: I am happy to meet you.
grammar: "Glad" + "att" + infinitive: happy to do something.

target: Vad roligt att du kunde komma!
english: How nice that you could come!
grammar: "Att" + past tense in exclamations expresses appreciation for completed actions.
"Hem" = home (direction). "Hemma" = at home (location).
"Tack för senast" is said when you see someone after a previous social gathering — very Swedish.

english: Say hi to everyone at home!

grammar: Group 1 verbs add -r in present tense: prata → pratar.
"Läsa" is Group 2. Present tense: läser. The -er ending is for Group 2.

english: We eat lunch at twelve o'clock.

target: De bor i ett stort hus.
english: They live in a big house.
grammar: "Bo" is Group 3: bor (present), bodde (past), bott (supine). Short and common.
"Jobba" is more casual than "arbeta." "Jobba" dominates everyday speech.

english: The children play in the park.
grammar: "Leka" = play (children). "Spela" = play (games/instruments/sports).

english: We drink coffee in the morning.
grammar: Swedish present covers both "I drink" and "I am drinking" — no continuous form.
Cycling is the primary transport for many Swedes, especially in Malmö and Uppsala.

english: We shop at the grocery store.
grammar: "Handla" is for groceries. "Shoppa" is for clothes/leisure shopping.
"Lyssna på" always requires "på" — you listen "on" something in Swedish.
"Spela" = play (instruments/games). "Leka" = play (children). Important distinction.
"Måla" = paint. "Måla" follows Group 1: målar, målade, målat.
"Börja" = start/begin. "Sluta" = finish/stop. Essential work vocabulary.
"Sluta" means both "stop" and "finish" in Swedish.
"Ringa" follows Group 2: ringer, ringde, ringt.
"Sälja" is irregular: säljer, sålde, sålt. Very common in daily life.
Sweden is one of the world's most cashless societies — card or Swish for most transactions.
"Tvätta" = wash. "Tvätt" = laundry. Group 1: tvättar, tvättade, tvättat.

english: They clean the house every week.

grammar: "Laga" means both "to cook" and "to fix/repair" — context makes it clear.
"Resa" is Group 2: reser, reste, rest. Norrland is Sweden's vast northern region.
"Flyga" is irregular: flyger, flög, flugit.
"Visa" = show. Group 1: visar, visade, visat.
"Bjuda in" = invite. "Bjuda" alone can mean treat/offer.

english: I work out at the gym.

grammar: "Fotografera" = photograph. Longer verbs often follow Group 1 (-ar).

english: I pick up the children from school.
grammar: "Hämta" means to fetch/pick up — very common in daily life with kids.

english: He drives a car to work.

grammar: Splitting bills is the norm in Sweden, not the exception.

english: I wake up early every morning.

grammar: "Äta" is a strong irregular verb: äter, åt, ätit. Among the most common.

target: Hon dricker te istället för kaffe.
english: She drinks tea instead of coffee.
I read the newspaper in the morning.
She is changing jobs next month.
grammar: Present tense can express near-future events, like English "She is changing..."

target: Vi flyttar till en ny stad.
english: We are moving to a new city.

grammar: "Fylla år" literally means "to fill years" — the Swedish idiom for having a birthday.

english: I take the bus to work.

grammar: Remote work is very common and accepted in Swedish work culture.
Recycling ("källsortering") is deeply embedded in Swedish culture.
"Behöva" = need. Group 2: behöver, behövde, behövt.
"Förstå" is strong/irregular: förstår, förstod, förstått.
"Naturen" is central to Swedish identity. Allemansrätten gives everyone the right to roam.
"Hoppas" is a deponent verb — ends in -s but has active meaning.

target: Hon tror att det blir regn.

grammar: "Veta" means to know a fact. "Känna" means to know a person or to feel.

target: Vi tycker att filmen var bra.
english: We think the movie was good.
grammar: "Tycka" = opinion. "Tänka" = think/ponder. "Tro" = believe/assume.
"Lova" = promise. Group 1: lovar, lovade, lovat.
"Bestämma sig" = decide. Reflexive — the "sig/mig" is essential.
"Glömma" is Group 2: glömmer, glömde, glömt.

english: She is looking for a new job.
grammar: "Söka" = search/apply. Group 2: söker, sökte, sökt.

english: We use the computer every day.
grammar: "Använda" = use. Group 2: använder, använde, använt.
"Sitt" = their own (reflexive possessive). "Deras" = someone else's.

english: We pick mushrooms in the forest.
grammar: Mushroom picking is a popular autumn activity, tied to allemansrätten.
"Fjällen" refers to the Scandinavian mountain range — popular for hiking and skiing.

english: I pick berries in the forest.
grammar: Berry picking is a cherished tradition protected by allemansrätten.
"Campa" is borrowed from English but fully integrated into Swedish grammar.
"Grilla" follows Group 1 like most newer/borrowed verbs: grillar, grillade, grillat.

english: She signs up for the course.

grammar: Winter tires are legally required in Sweden from December 1 to March 31.
"Bergen" is the definite plural of "berg" (mountain).

target: Vi möter våra vänner på lördag.
english: We meet our friends on Saturday.

grammar: "Vara" (to be) is used for states: "Jag är glad," "Jag är trött."
No article before professions: "Hon är läkare," not "Hon är en läkare."
"Hemma" = at home (location). "Hem" = home (direction): "Vi går hem."
"Glad" becomes "glada" in plural. Adjectives agree in number.
"Ha" is irregular: har (present), hade (past), haft (supine).
Swedish expresses pain as "ha ont i" + body part.
"Ha det bra" means "to be doing well." Also used as a goodbye.
"Det" serves as impersonal subject for weather: "Det regnar," "Det snöar."
"Imorgon" is one word in Swedish (unlike English "tomorrow").
"Bäst" = best. The superlative of "bra" — completely irregular.
"Katt" is an en-word. Plural: katter. Definite plural: katterna.
July is the main vacation month. Many businesses slow down or close.
Swedish uses "vara" (be) for hunger and thirst: "Jag är hungrig/törstig."
"Inte" is placed after the verb in main clauses.
"Ha rätt" = be right. "Ha fel" = be wrong. Swedish uses "have" where English uses "be."
"Fråga" is both a noun (en fråga) and a verb (att fråga). Very common.
"Vacker" = beautiful. Used for scenery, nature. "Fin" is more casual/general.
"Ha fullt upp" = to be very busy / have a lot on one's plate.
"Nyfiken" = curious. Literally "new-eager" — a compound adjective.
"Ha bråttom" = to be in a hurry — an untranslatable idiom.
"Redo" is invariable — same form for en/ett/plural: "Han är redo," "De är redo."
"Möjligt" takes -t because "det" is neuter. "Det är möjligt" vs "saken är möjlig."
"Sjuk" = sick. Pred. adjective: "Han är sjuk" vs "den sjuka mannen" (def.).
"Ikväll" = tonight/this evening. One word, like "imorgon" (tomorrow).
"Fint" has -t because "hus" is ett-word: "ett fint hus."
"Rädd för" = afraid of. "Rädd om" = careful with / caring about.

target: Det är viktigt att komma i tid.
english: It is important to come on time.

grammar: "Ha tur" = be lucky. "Ha otur" = be unlucky.
"Ha lust" = feel like / want to — very common in casual Swedish.
"Stressad" is from English "stressed" — adapted to Swedish adjective rules.

english: They have problems with the computer.
grammar: "Problem" is ett-word: ett problem, problemet. Same form singular/plural.
"Ingen fara" is a reassuring phrase meaning "no worries."

english: We are satisfied with the result.
grammar: "Nöjd" = satisfied. Plural: "nöjda." A very useful adjective.
"Erfarenhet" = experience. En-word ending in -het (suffix for abstract nouns).

target: Hon är duktig på att laga mat.
grammar: "Duktig på" = good at — very common in Swedish.

english: They are tired after the trip.
grammar: "Trött" becomes "trötta" in plural: "trötta" for multiple people.

target: Det är skönt att vara ledig.
grammar: "Skönt" conveys comfort and relief — a very Swedish sensation word.

target: Vi har en stuga i Dalarna.
english: We have a cabin in Dalarna.
grammar: Having a "stuga" (cottage) is a Swedish dream. Many families have one.
"I" for being in a city/country. "Till" for going to a city/country.

target: De har två hundar och en katt.
english: They have two dogs and a cat.
We are having a cozy time.
grammar: "Ha det mysigt" captures the concept of togetherness and coziness.
"Tradition" is an en-word. Most abstract -tion words are en-words.

english: He has control of the situation.

grammar: "Ha svårt att" = find it difficult to — very useful expression.
"Det är dags" = it's time — very commonly used.

target: Jag har ett möte klockan tre.
english: I have a meeting at three o'clock.

grammar: "Ha det lugnt" = to take it easy. "Lugnt" is neuter because of impersonal "det."
"Förkyld" literally means "over-cold" — the Swedish word for having a cold.

english: She is having a good day.

target: Det är dyrt att bo i Stockholm.
english: It is expensive to live in Stockholm.

target: Vi är gifta sedan tio år.
english: We have been married for ten years.

grammar: "Ha roligt" = have fun. "Roligt" alone = funny or fun.
"Ingen/inget/inga" = no/none. Inga = plural: "inga planer, inga pengar."
"Försenad" means delayed/late — useful for public transport.
"Chef" in Swedish means boss/manager, not cook. "Kock" = cook/chef.

english: We have been friends since childhood.
grammar: "Sedan" = since. "Barndom" = childhood. -en makes it definite.
"Pigg" = alert, energetic — no direct English equivalent. Very Swedish.
"Dröm" is an en-word: en dröm, drömmen, drömmar, drömmarna.

english: We have a shortage of time.

grammar: "Lagom" is the quintessential Swedish concept — not too much, not too little.

target: Vi har hela helgen på oss.

grammar: "Underbar" = wonderful. "Under" = wonder/miracle in old Swedish.

target: Jag har en känsla av att det ordnar sig.
english: I have a feeling it will work out.

target: Vi har rätt att vara arga.
english: We have the right to be angry.
We are grateful for the help.

grammar: "Ha gott om" = have plenty of — the opposite of "ha brist på."

english: A book is on the table.
grammar: "En" is for common gender (en-words). "Ett" is for neuter (ett-words).
"Äpple" is an ett-word. You must learn gender with each noun — no reliable rule.
Definite form: add -en/-n to en-words: "en bok" → "boken."
Definite form: add -et/-t to ett-words: "ett äpple" → "äpplet."
"Bil" is en-word: en bil, bilen, bilar, bilarna.
"Hus" is ett: "ett hus" → "huset." Adjective takes -t: "stor" → "stort."

english: A cat is sitting in the window.

grammar: "Barn" is ett: "ett barn" → "barnet."

target: Jag har en vän i Malmö.
english: I have a friend in Malmö.

grammar: "Vatten" is ett. "Kallt" has -t to agree with neuter gender.
"Stol" is en-word, "bord" is ett-word. There's no pattern — just memorize them.
"Flicka" → "flickan" — the -n ending for en-words ending in -a.

target: Vi har ett rum med utsikt.
english: We have a room with a view.
grammar: "Rum" is ett-word: ett rum, rummet, rum, rummen.
"Kopp" is en-word. Most kitchen items: en tallrik, en gaffel, en sked.

english: The forest is beautiful in autumn.

grammar: "Glas" is ett-word: ett glas, glaset, glas, glasen.

english: The woman waits for the bus.

grammar: "Blomma" is en-word ending in -a. Definite: blomman.
"Norra" = northern. Compass adj: norra, södra, östra, västra.
"Hav" is ett: "ett hav" → "havet."
"Bio" = cinema. "Gå på bio" = go to the movies. Always "på," not "till."
"Skrivbord" = desk. Compound: "skriv" (write) + "bord" (table).
"Dag" is en-word: en dag, dagen, dagar, dagarna.
"Trädgård" literally means "tree-yard" — an en-word.

english: A country with a lot of forest.

grammar: "Strand" is en-word: en strand, stranden, stränder, stränderna.
"Bro" is en-word. "Älv" is en-word (large river).

english: The museum closes at five o'clock.

grammar: "By" is en-word: en by, byn, byar, byarna. Means village.

english: The square is full of people.
grammar: "Torg" is ett: "ett torg" → "torget."
"Nyckel" is en-word: en nyckel, nyckeln, nycklar, nycklarna.
"Väg" is en-word: en väg, vägen, vägar, vägarna.
"Hörn" is ett-word: ett hörn, hörnet. Means corner.
"Present" means gift — a false friend with English "present" (time).
"Väder" is ett: "ett väder" → "vädret."
In December in Stockholm, darkness falls as early as 3 PM.
Red wooden houses ("röda stugor") are iconic, painted with Falu red pigment.
Plural definite: "fönster" → "fönstren" (the windows).

english: The books are on the shelf.
grammar: Plural definite: "böcker" → "böckerna."
Plural definite: "barn" → "barnen."
"Med" for means of transport: med tåg, med buss, med flyg.
"Tallrik" is en-word: en tallrik, tallriken.
"Bit" = piece. "En bit av" = a piece of.
"Sked" (spoon) and "gaffel" (fork) are both en-words.

english: The butter is in the fridge.

grammar: "Flaska" is en-word: en flaska, flaskan. Definite adds -n to -a words.
V2 rule: the verb MUST be second. When a non-subject starts, the subject moves after the verb.
"Igår" first → "regnade" second → "det" third. This is V2 inversion.
"Ofta" in first position forces inversion: "dricker jag" not "jag dricker." V2 rule.

target: I Stockholm bor det många människor.
grammar: "Det" is an expletive (filler) subject. The real subject follows the verb.

target: Varje dag går jag till jobbet.
english: Every day I walk to work.
grammar: Time expressions at front trigger V2: "Varje dag" (1st) + "går" (2nd) + "jag" (3rd).
"Nu" at front triggers V2: Nu + börjar + det (subject after verb).
"Här" triggers inversion: "Här + kan + man" instead of "Man kan här."

target: Aldrig har jag sett något sådant.
english: Never have I seen anything like it.
grammar: Starting with "aldrig" creates emphasis and triggers V2 inversion.
"Kanske" can trigger V2: "kanske kommer han." But "han kanske kommer" is also fine.

target: Jag läser en bok varje kväll.
english: I read a book every evening.
grammar: Standard SVO order when subject starts — no inversion needed.

target: I morse åt vi frukost tidigt.
english: This morning we ate breakfast early.
grammar: "I morse" = this morning. Past tense because the morning has passed.

target: Förra veckan reste vi till Kiruna.
english: Last week we traveled to Kiruna.
grammar: "Förra" = last: förra veckan, förra året, förra gången.
"Där borta" = over there. Place adverb first → verb second → subject third.
"Snart" = soon. Fronted adverb triggers V2 inversion.

target: Ibland går vi ut och äter.
english: Sometimes we go out to eat.
grammar: "Ibland" = sometimes. Triggers V2 inversion like other fronted adverbs.

target: På kvällen tittar vi på TV.
english: In the evening we watch TV.
grammar: "På kvällen" = in the evening. Time phrases with "på" for parts of the day.

target: I helgen ska vi åka till stugan.
english: On the weekend we're going to the cabin.
grammar: "Stugan" = the cottage. Many Swedes have a "stuga" for weekends and holidays.

target: Först äter vi, sedan går vi.
english: First we eat, then we go.

grammar: "Dessutom" = moreover. Sentence adverb — triggers V2.

english: Already at six o'clock I woke up.
grammar: "Redan" = already. When fronted, V2 rule applies.

target: Under sommaren badar vi i sjön.
english: During the summer we swim in the lake.
grammar: "Under" + time = during: "under sommaren" = during summer.
Even single adverbs trigger V2: "Plötsligt" (1st) + "stannade" (2nd).

english: In Sweden the summer is short.
grammar: Place phrases trigger V2: "I Sverige" (1st) + "är" (2nd) + "sommaren" (3rd).

english: In the middle of the night I woke up.

grammar: In questions with a question word, V2 still applies: question word + verb + subject.
When the question word IS the subject, no inversion: Vem + verb.

english: Which movie do you want to see?

target: Ju mer jag läser, desto bättre förstår jag.
english: The more I read, the better I understand.
grammar: "Ju ... desto" is Swedish for "the more ... the more."

target: Sällan ser man norrsken i södra Sverige.
english: Seldom do you see northern lights in southern Sweden.
Behind the house there is a garden.
grammar: "Finns" = exists/there is. "Det finns" = there is/are.

english: In front of the school the bus stops.
grammar: Prepositional phrases count as one unit in first position for V2.

english: Next to the bank is the pharmacy.
Despite the rain we went out.
According to the newspaper it's true.
At eight o'clock the lesson starts.
Outside the door a taxi is waiting.
Through the park runs a path.
Around the lake there are hiking trails.

target: Bland alla elever är hon bäst.
english: Among all students she is the best.
Between the houses there is an alley.
From here you can see the whole city.

target: Inte förrän imorgon kan jag svara.
english: Not until tomorrow can I answer.

target: Bara på sommaren är det så varmt.
english: Only in summer is it this warm.

target: Fortfarande bor hon i samma stad.
english: She still lives in the same city.

target: Hellre cyklar jag än kör bil.
english: I'd rather cycle than drive a car.
After all it turned out well.

target: På grund av regnet ställdes matchen in.
english: Because of the rain the match was cancelled.
So far everything has gone well.

target: Visserligen är det dyrt, men det är värt det.
english: Admittedly it's expensive, but it's worth it.

target: Så fort jag kom hem somnade jag.
english: As soon as I got home I fell asleep.

target: Normalt sett äter vi klockan sex.
I alla fall är vi framme nu.
english: In any case we are here now.

target: Med andra ord ska vi vänta.
english: In other words we should wait.

target: I samband med flytten bytte hon jobb.
english: In connection with the move she changed jobs.

target: I stället för att klaga borde vi agera.
english: Instead of complaining we should act.

target: Till en början var det svårt.
I slutändan spelar det ingen roll.
english: In the end it doesn't matter.

target: Hur som helst måste vi gå nu.
I värsta fall får vi vänta.
english: In the worst case we'll have to wait.

target: I bästa fall är vi framme klockan tre.
english: In the best case we'll arrive at three.

target: Med tanke på vädret stannar vi inne.
english: Considering the weather we'll stay inside.

target: Senast igår pratade jag med honom.
english: Just yesterday I spoke with him.

target: Sedan dess har jag inte hört något.
english: Since then I have not heard anything.

target: Under tiden kan du vänta här.
english: In the meantime you can wait here.
Daily she walks in the park.
Below the mountain lies a village.
Above the clouds the sun shines.

target: Å ena sidan förstår jag dig.
english: On one hand I understand you.

target: Å andra sidan har han rätt.
english: On the other hand he is right.

target: Nog kan du stanna lite till.
english: Surely you can stay a bit longer.

target: Särskilt på vintern är det mörkt.
english: Especially in winter it is dark.
More and more often people work from home.

target: Utöver det har vi inga problem.
english: Besides that we have no problems.

target: Lite senare kom resten av gruppen.
english: A little later the rest of the group arrived.

target: Strax före midnatt gick vi hem.
english: Just before midnight we went home.

target: Som tur var hittade vi biljetten.
Även i Sverige snöar det inte alltid.
english: Even in Sweden it doesn't always snow.

target: Hemma hos oss firar vi alltid jul.
english: At our home we always celebrate Christmas.
Inside the gate the children play.

target: Knappt hade vi kommit förrän det began regna.
english: We had barely arrived when it started raining.

target: Inte bara regnade det, det blåste också.
english: Not only did it rain, it was also windy.
grammar: "Inte bara...också" (not only...also). First clause inverts; second is normal order.
"Klockan" means both "the clock" and "o'clock." Swedish uses 24-hour time formally.

english: We'll meet at half past four.
grammar: "Halv fem" means half TO five (4:30), not half past five. A common trap for learners!
Swedish numbers 21-99 put the tens first: tjugofem = twenty-five. No hyphen needed.
"Barn" is an ett-word that stays the same in plural: ett barn, två barn.

english: We live on the third floor.
grammar: Ordinal numbers: "första, andra, tredje" — the first three are irregular.

english: It is the fifteenth of March.
grammar: Ordinal dates use -e or -te endings: femtonde = fifteenth. Swedish dates go day-month.

english: It is a quarter past nine.
grammar: "Kvart över" = quarter past. "Kvart i" = quarter to.

english: I turn thirty years old tomorrow.
grammar: "Fylla år" literally means 'fill years' — the standard way to say 'have a birthday.'
"Det finns" (there is/are) works for both singular and plural in Swedish.

target: Vi har väntat i tjugo minuter.
english: We have waited for twenty minutes.
grammar: "I" + time period = "for": "i tjugo minuter" (for 20 minutes). Don't use "för" here.
Written timetables always use 24-hour format in Sweden.
Swedish numbers 1-5: en/ett, två, tre, fyra, fem. "En" for en-words, "ett" for ett-words.
"Sju" (7) contains the tricky sj-sound [ɧ]. "Åtta" has double-t making the å short.
13-19 add "-ton" (like English "-teen"): tretton, fjorton, femton, sexton...
"Arton" is the modern form of 18 (not "aderton" which is formal/written).
Tens use "-tio" suffix: trettio (30), fyrtio (40). Note "fyrtio" not "förtio".
"Hundra" (100) is an ett-word: ett hundra, but you usually drop "ett" before it.

target: Tusen kronor är inte så mycket.
english: A thousand kronor isn't that much.
grammar: "Tusen" = thousand. No plural: "två tusen," not "två tusens."
"Miljon" is an en-word: en miljon, miljonen. "Invånare" = inhabitant — same singular and plural.
"Första" = first. Ordinals 1-3 are irregular: första, andra, tredje.
Days of the week are not capitalized in Swedish.

english: Sunday is a day of rest.

grammar: Months are not capitalized in Swedish: januari, februari, mars.

english: March is the beginning of spring.

grammar: Midsummer falls on the Friday between June 19-25. Not always the 24th.

target: Vi äter lunch klockan halv ett.
english: We eat lunch at half past twelve.
The meeting lasts for an hour.
It takes a quarter of an hour.

target: En halvtimme senare var vi framme.
english: Half an hour later we arrived.

target: Det är bara två dagar kvar.
english: There are only two days left.
In three weeks we are leaving.

target: Det var för fem år sedan.
Jag har bott här i åtta månader.
english: I have lived here for eight months.

target: Under de senaste tio åren har mycket förändrats.
english: In the last ten years a lot has changed.

target: Det finns 365 dagar på ett år.
english: There are 365 days in a year.
grammar: "På" used idiomatically: "dagar på ett år" = days in a year.

english: The temperature is minus ten degrees.
It has been snowing for two hours.

grammar: "Ägg" is ett-word: ett ägg, ägget. Same form singular and plural.

target: Boka ett bord för fyra personer.
english: Book a table for four people.

target: Dubbelt så mycket som förra gången.
english: Twice as much as last time.

target: Vi åker om en och en halv timme.
english: We are leaving in an hour and a half.

target: Det kostar sexhundra kronor per natt.
english: It costs six hundred kronor per night.
The apartment is seventy square meters.

target: Flyget tar en timme och trettio minuter.
english: The flight takes one hour and thirty minutes.
We arrive at half past seven.

target: Jag vaknar klockan sex varje morgon.
english: I wake up at six every morning.
We need at least three days.

target: Det finns tjugotre elever i klassen.
english: There are twenty-three students in the class.

target: Vi firar tvåhundra år av fred.
english: We celebrate two hundred years of peace.
The bill was eight hundred kronor.

target: Siffran noll är viktig i matematik.
english: The number zero is important in math.

target: Hastigheten är nittio kilometer i timmen.
english: The speed is ninety kilometers per hour.
The population is approximately ten million.

target: Varje lektion varar i fyrtiofem minuter.
Vi har handlat för trehundra kronor.
english: We have shopped for three hundred kronor.
It is the nineteenth of August.

target: Första maj är en röd dag i Sverige.
english: May Day is a public holiday in Sweden.
grammar: "Röd dag" (red day) is the Swedish term for public holidays.

target: Klockan tolv på natten är det midnatt.
english: At twelve at night it is midnight.

target: Klockan tolv på dagen är det mitt på dagen.
english: At twelve during the day it is noon.

target: Vi har tjugofyra timmar på oss.

english: The bus comes every ten minutes.

target: Det är sextio sekunder på en minut.
english: There are sixty seconds in a minute.

target: Om ett par timmar är vi framme.
english: In a couple of hours we'll be there.
It is the third of December.

target: Den trettonde december firar vi Lucia.
english: On the thirteenth of December we celebrate Lucia.
grammar: Lucia is a beloved Swedish tradition with candlelit processions and singing.

english: We'll see each other in two weeks.
One hundred percent of students participated.

target: Det tog oss sex timmar att köra dit.
english: It took us six hours to drive there.

target: En tredjedel av befolkningen bor i Stockholm.
english: A third of the population lives in Stockholm.
We need at least forty minutes.

target: Jag har elva dagar kvar av semestern.
english: I have eleven days left of vacation.

target: Det finns fyra årstider i Sverige.
english: There are four seasons in Sweden.
The car costs two hundred thousand kronor.

target: Jag betalar femtio kronor per månad.
english: I pay fifty kronor per month.

target: Barnen har lov i två veckor.
english: The children have vacation for two weeks.

target: Jag vill ha en kopp kaffe, tack.
english: I would like a cup of coffee, please.
grammar: "Jag vill ha" is the polite way to order. Literally "I want to have."

english: Can I have the bill, please?
grammar: "Kan jag få" is more polite than "Jag vill ha" when ordering.

target: Vi skulle vilja boka ett bord för två.
english: We would like to book a table for two.
grammar: "Vi skulle vilja" is the most formal way to make a request.
"Rekommendera" is borrowed from French/Latin — common in Swedish.

target: Jag tar en lax med potatis.
grammar: "Jag tar" (I'll take) is the most natural way to order at a restaurant.
"Ni" is used for addressing service staff, though "du" is also fine.

target: En öl och en glass, tack.
english: A beer and an ice cream, please.

target: Kan vi få lite mer bröd?
english: Can we get some more bread?

grammar: "Smörgås" is quintessentially Swedish — an open-faced sandwich with various toppings.

target: Vi tar en fika på kafét.
english: We'll have fika at the café.
grammar: "Kafé" is ett-word. "Kafét" = the café (definite).
Köttbullar med lingon is perhaps Sweden's most iconic dish.

english: Breakfast is included in the price.
grammar: "Ingå i" = be included in. Common for hotel descriptions.

english: The coffee is strong and good.

target: Jag tar en kanelbulle till kaffet.
english: I'll have a cinnamon bun with the coffee.
grammar: Kanelbulle (cinnamon bun) is essential to Swedish fika culture.
"Kort" = card. Sweden is nearly cashless — cards accepted everywhere.
Tipping is not expected in Sweden — service charge is usually included.

english: We eat dinner at six o'clock.
grammar: "Middag" = dinner (evening meal). Lunch is the midday meal.

english: The soup is warm and good.

target: Vi tar en dessert att dela.
english: We'll have a dessert to share.
Which soup do you have today?

target: Jag skulle vilja ha en glass.
english: I would like an ice cream.
We eat breakfast at seven o'clock.
I'll have vegetables with the main course.
A cup of tea with honey.

target: Vi äter surströmming en gång om året.
english: We eat surströmming once a year.
grammar: Surströmming (fermented herring) is notoriously smelly — eaten outdoors in late summer.

english: Do you have a children's menu?

target: Jag vill ha lite grädde till kaffet.
english: I would like some cream for the coffee.
The herring at the Christmas table is traditional.
grammar: Julbordet (Christmas buffet) features many varieties of pickled herring.

english: The pancakes are good with jam.
grammar: Thursday is traditionally pancake day ("pannkakstorsdag") in Sweden.

english: We'll order home delivery pizza tonight.
Juicy apple pie with vanilla sauce.

target: Jag tar en macka till lunch.
english: I'll have a sandwich for lunch.
The crayfish party is in August.
grammar: Kräftskiva is a beloved Swedish tradition — eating crayfish with dill, singing songs.

english: We drink mulled wine in winter.
grammar: Glögg is the Swedish mulled wine, spiced and served with almonds and raisins.

target: Smöret och osten ligger på bordet.
english: The butter and cheese are on the table.

target: Jag vill ha socker i teet.
english: I want sugar in the tea.
The starter is a tuna salad.
The main course is grilled chicken.
The dessert is a chocolate cake.

target: Buffén har mycket att välja på.
english: The buffet has a lot to choose from.
I eat oatmeal porridge every morning.
grammar: Havregrynsgröt is a classic Swedish breakfast, often with milk and jam.

english: Soured milk tastes good with cereal.
grammar: Filmjölk is a fermented dairy product unique to Scandinavia.

english: We grill sausages by the lake.

target: En bit ost och en skiva bröd.
english: A piece of cheese and a slice of bread.
We drink schnapps with the herring.
grammar: Singing snapsvisor (drinking songs) while toasting is a Swedish tradition.

english: Small sausages are served at the Christmas table.
I'll have a sticky chocolate cake.
grammar: Kladdkaka is a beloved Swedish dessert — intentionally undercooked for gooey richness.

target: Semlan är en favorit i februari.
english: The semla is a favorite in February.
grammar: Semlor (cream-filled buns) are traditionally eaten before Lent, now available January-March.

english: Saffron buns are baked for Lucia.

target: Vi har alltid pepparkakor till jul.
english: We always have gingerbread cookies for Christmas.

target: Jag dricker en smoothie efter träningen.
english: I drink a smoothie after the workout.
The hot dog with bread and mustard.

grammar: "Fredagsmys" (cozy Friday) often features tacos — a uniquely Swedish tradition.

english: Do you have a vegan option?
Pea soup is served on Thursdays.
grammar: Ärtsoppa on Thursdays is an old Swedish military tradition still kept alive.

target: Jag tar en portion pommes frites.
english: I'll have a portion of French fries.
The bread with butter and cheese.
A bottle of mineral water, please.
We'll each have a glass of wine.
Cider is popular in the summer.
The fish gratin is the house specialty.

grammar: Toast Skagen is a Swedish appetizer with shrimp, mayo, and dill on toast.

english: We eat potato pancake with pork.
Jansson's Temptation for the Christmas table.
grammar: A classic Swedish potato and anchovy casserole served at Christmas.

english: The strawberry cake is served at midsummer.
We'll bring out the cheese board.
The sandwich with shrimp and mayonnaise.

target: Vi fikar minst två gånger om dagen.
english: We have fika at least twice a day.
The soured milk is served with crispbread.
grammar: Knäckebröd (crispbread) is a Swedish staple found in every household.
"Det gör inget" is the go-to phrase for "it doesn't matter" or "no worries."
"Ingen orsak" literally means "no cause" — used like "you're welcome" or "no problem."
"Vad + adjective/noun" = what a...! "Vad synd!" = what a pity! Very common exclamation.
"Vad bra!" = how great! Swedish uses "vad" (what) for exclamations, not "hur" (how).
"Veta" is irregular: vet (present), visste (past), vetat (supinum). "Vet inte" = don't know.
"Spela roll" = matter. "Ingen roll" = no matter/doesn't matter.
"Bero på" = depend on. Very commonly used in conversation.
"Javisst" combines "ja" and "visst" (certainly). Very emphatic agreement.
"Självklart" = of course! Compound: "själv" (self) + "klart" (clear). Very common.
"Aning" = idea/inkling. "Ingen aning" = no idea.
"Stämma" means to be correct or to match — used to confirm information.
Swedes often say "det ordnar sig" to express calm optimism about the future.
"Mena allvar" = be serious. "Allvar" = seriousness.

english: It was a long time ago.
grammar: "Länge sedan" = long ago. "Det var länge sedan vi sågs" = it's been a long time.
"Var så snäll" literally means "be so kind" — a polite way to ask for something.
"Ta det lugnt" is one of the most common Swedish expressions for "calm down" or "relax."
"Få" here means "will have to" — a softer future than "ska."
"Längta" expresses yearning or longing — a deeply Swedish word with no exact English match.
"Äntligen" = finally/at last. Expresses relief after a long wait.
"Tråkigt" means both "boring" and "sad/too bad" depending on context.
"Fatta" is casual for understanding. More formal: "förstå."
Mildly vulgar but extremely common in casual Swedish.
"Lagom" — not too good, not too bad, just the right amount.

target: Det var det sista jag väntade mig.
english: That was the last thing I expected.

target: Man lär sig så länge man lever.
english: You learn as long as you live.

grammar: "Ingen ko på isen" means no worries — a distinctly Swedish idiom.

target: Jag ser det som en möjlighet.
english: I see it as an opportunity.

target: Vi tar det som det kommer.
english: We'll take it as it comes.

target: Jag tänkte att det var du.
Jag kommer så fort jag kan.
english: I'll come as soon as I can.

grammar: "Mätt" means satisfied/full after eating — important at Swedish dinner parties.
Mildly vulgar exclamation expressing astonishment.

english: Right is right / Fair is fair.
How does one go about it?

grammar: "Lagom är bäst" — the unofficial Swedish motto. Just the right amount is ideal.

english: I went to the store yesterday.
grammar: "Gå" is irregular in past tense: gå → gick. Many common verbs are irregular.
"Äta" → "åt" in past tense. One of the most common irregular verbs.

target: Vi reste till Gotland förra sommaren.
english: We traveled to Gotland last summer.
grammar: Group 2 verbs: -te ending in past: resa → reste.

english: He played football as a child.
grammar: Group 1 verbs: -ade ending in past: spela → spelade.

target: De bodde i Lund i tio år.
english: They lived in Lund for ten years.

grammar: "Dricka" → "drack." Irregular — the vowel changes like English drink/drank.

target: Vi såg en fin film igår.
english: We saw a nice movie yesterday.
grammar: "Se" → "såg." Another common irregular verb.
"Köpa" is Group 2: köper, köpte, köpt. Past: -te ending.
"Läsa" is Group 2: läser, läste, läst.

target: Han skrev ett brev till sin mamma.
english: He wrote a letter to his mom.
grammar: "Skriva" is strong: skriver, skrev, skrivit. Vowel changes.
"Sjunga" is strong: sjunger, sjöng, sjungit. i-ö-u pattern.
"Sova" is strong: sover, sov, sovit.
"Hitta" is Group 1: hittar, hittade, hittat.
"Ha" → "hade" in past tense. Irregular but very common.
"Vara" → "var" in past tense. The most basic irregular verb.
"Bli" → "blev" is used like English "became" but also for "got" (surprised, angry, etc.).

target: Jag lärde mig att simma som liten.
english: I learned to swim when I was little.

grammar: "Sluta" = stop/finish. Group 1: slutar, slutade, slutat.

english: We went skiing in the mountains.
grammar: "Åka skidor" = go skiing. "Åka" → "åkte" (Group 2).

target: Han stod i kön i en timme.
english: He stood in line for an hour.
grammar: "Stå" → "stod." Swedes are known for being orderly queue-standers.

target: Jag satt på bussen i trettio minuter.
english: I sat on the bus for thirty minutes.

grammar: "Få" → "fick" (got). Used very frequently in Swedish.

english: We took the train to Malmö.

target: Hon sa att hon var trött.
grammar: "Säga" → "sa" — short and irregular.

target: Jag trodde att du var hemma.
english: I thought you were at home.

grammar: "Vakna" = wake up (intransitive). "Väcka" = wake someone (transitive).
"Le" → "log." A short, irregular verb.
"Gråta" → "grät" — an irregular strong verb.

target: Vi bestämde oss för att stanna.
grammar: "Bestämma sig för" = decide on. The preposition "för" is required.
"Lägga märke till" = notice. A three-part particle verb.

target: Hon kände lukten av nybakat bröd.
english: She smelled the scent of freshly baked bread.
grammar: "Nybakat" = freshly baked. Compound adj: "ny" + past participle "bakat."

english: I carried the bag the whole way.
We set up camp by the lake.

grammar: "Fara" → "for" — an old Swedish verb for traveling, still commonly used.
"Frysa" → "frös" means to freeze/be cold.

english: They got seats on the train.
We arrived late in the evening.
We checked in at the hotel.

grammar: Object pronouns: mig (me), dig (you), honom (him), henne (her), oss (us), er (you pl.), dem (them).
Indirect objects (mig, dig, etc.) come before direct objects: "gav mig en present."
"Dem" is the object form of "de" (they). In speech, both are often pronounced "dom."
Object pronouns: mig, dig, honom, henne, oss, er, dem. "Henne" = her (object).

english: Can you give me the salt?
grammar: Object pronouns go right after the verb: "ge mig," "hjälp mig."
"Er" is both 'you' (object, plural) and 'your.' Context determines the meaning.
"Oss" = us. Object form of "vi" (we).
Many Swedish verbs need a preposition with their object: vänta PÅ (wait for).
"Dig" = you (object). Pronounced "dej" in everyday speech.

english: We visited him at the hospital.
grammar: "Honom" is the object form of "han." Swedish distinguishes subject/object pronouns clearly.

target: Kan du hjälpa mig med det här?
english: Can you help me with this?
grammar: "Det här" (this) vs. "det där" (that) — "här" and "där" add proximity.
"Titta på" = look at. Object pronouns after prepositions: på mig, på dig, på oss, på dem.
"Dem" is pronounced "dom" in speech, as is "de" (they).
"Inte" goes after the object pronoun: "hörde dig inte."
"Be om" = ask for. "Bad" is past of "be." Indirect object (henne) before "om."
"Er" is both "you" (object, plural) and "your." In speech, "er" as object is being replaced by "dig" (singular).
In imperatives, the pronoun follows directly: "Följ mig," "Ring honom."
"Honom" = him. Object form of "han" (he).
"Berätta för" = tell (to someone). "Det" (it) as object goes before "för oss."
"Ni" = you (plural subject). "Er" = you (plural object). "Mig" is often pronounced "mej."

english: I met her at the party.
grammar: "Träffa" = meet. Object pronouns go right after the verb: "träffade henne."
Indirect object (oss) before direct object (ett paket): "skickade oss ett paket."
"Bjuda på" = treat to / invite for. "Bjöd" is past of "bjuda" (strong verb).
"Lita på" = to trust. The preposition "på" is required.

english: He asked me about the way.
They thank you for the help.

grammar: "Bry sig om" = to care about. A reflexive verb with a preposition.

english: We saw them at the square.
She picked me up at the station.
I followed him to the door.

target: Jag presenterade honom för mina föräldrar.
english: I introduced him to my parents.
He gave me his phone number.

target: Vi bjöd in dem till festen.
english: We invited them to the party.
I saw her in the store.
She made me happy with the news.

grammar: "Förlåta" → "förlät" in past tense. Forgiveness is important in Swedish culture.
"Tro på" = believe in. Different from "tro" alone which means to think/assume.

english: He reminded me about the meeting.

target: Jag ber dig om en tjänst.
english: I ask you for a favor.
grammar: "Be om en tjänst" = ask for a favor. "Tjänst" also means service/job.

english: We entrusted her with the secret.

target: Han envisades med att hjälpa mig.

english: We took them to the hotel.

grammar: "Kan" expresses ability. The main verb is always in infinitive: kan + simma.
"Måste" has no infinitive form — it's always "måste" in all tenses.

english: We are going to go to the cabin.
grammar: "Ska" expresses intention, plan, or future. Very frequently used.
"Vill" = want. Unlike English, no "to" needed: "vill lära sig" not "vill att lära sig."
"Borde" = should/ought to. It implies advice or mild obligation.
"Får" = may/allowed to. "Får inte" = must not / not allowed to.
"Behöva" = need. Can be followed by infinitive or a noun.
Modal + inte + infinitive: the negation goes between the modal and main verb.
"Ska" + infinitive expresses plans or obligations. "Oroa sig" is reflexive (worry oneself).
"Måste" (must) never changes form — same for all persons, all tenses.
"Vill ha" = want (a thing). Just "vill" = want (to do). Don't forget "ha" with nouns.
"Kan" means both 'can' and 'know how to.' No 'att' before the infinitive after modals.

english: We are meeting at five o'clock.
grammar: "Ska" for plans/arrangements: "Vi ska träffas" = we're meeting (arranged).
"Man" = one/you (impersonal). "Måste" is invariable — no past tense form; use "var tvungen att" for past.
"Får inte" = not allowed to. "Får" also means "get/receive" — context determines meaning.
"Borde" = should (present advice, though technically past of "böra"). "Vila sig" = rest (reflexive).
"Behöver inte" = don't need to. Much milder than "måste inte" (must not). Important distinction.

english: He wants to move to Spain.
grammar: "Vill" = want to. No "att" after modals: "vill flytta" not "vill att flytta."
"Kan" = can/know how to. No "att" needed: "kan hjälpa" = can help.

english: We are going to celebrate with cake.
grammar: "Ska" for intentions: "ska fira" = going to celebrate. "Tårta" = cake (layer cake specifically).
"Övertid" = overtime. "Jobba" is informal for "arbeta" — both mean work.

target: Jag vill inte gå ut ikväll.
english: I don't want to go out tonight.

grammar: "Kan du" + infinitive is the standard polite request format.

english: Do you want to come along?

grammar: "Får jag" = may I. Polite but not overly formal.

english: Do I need to bring something?

target: Jag kan inte hitta mina nycklar.

english: We are going to travel to Japan.
She wants to become a pilot.
You can swim for free here.

target: Han kan laga mat riktigt bra.
Vi ska gifta oss nästa år.
english: We are going to get married next year.

grammar: "Inte" goes after the verb in main clauses: subject + verb + inte.
Main clause negation: subject + verb + inte. Simple and clear.
"Inte" after "har": "Vi har inte." Always after the finite verb.
"Inte längre" = no longer / not anymore. "Längre" = longer/anymore.
With modal verbs, "inte" goes after the modal: vill + inte + infinitive.
"Kan inte" = can't. Negation always after the modal verb in main clauses.

grammar: "Ska inte" = won't / aren't going to. "Länge" = for a long time.
"Behöver inte" = doesn't need to. Milder than "ska inte" (won't).
"Aldrig" (never) goes in the same position as "inte" — after the verb.
"Ingenting" = nothing. Also written "inget" in casual speech.
"Ingen" = nobody/no one. "Inget" for ett-words, "inga" for plural.
In perfect tense, "inte" goes between "har" and the supine: har + inte + sett.

grammar: En-words take the base adjective form: "en stor bil," "en fin dag."
Ett-words take -t: "stor" → "stort." "Fin" → "fint."
Plural takes -a: "stor" → "stora." Same for both en- and ett-words.
Definite adjective takes -a AND needs "den/det/de": "den stora bilen." This is the double definite.
Predicate adjectives agree with the subject: "Bilen är stor" (en-word), "Huset är stort" (ett-word).
Simple definite: "Huset" = the house. Suffix -et for ett-words.
Predicative plural: always -a form: "Bilarna är stora."
"Lång" = tall/long. Comparative: längre. Superlative: längst.
"Stark" = strong. Regular comparison: starkare, starkast.
"Liten" is irregular: liten (en), litet (ett), lilla/lilla (def.), små (plural).
"Liten" → "små" in plural. One of the most irregular adjectives.
"Vacker" = beautiful. Becomes "vackert" for ett-words, "vackra" for plural.
"Varm" → "varmt" for ett-words. The -t makes it neuter.
Comparative: add -re. "Stor" → "större." "Fin" → "finare."

target: Vi tog omvägen för utsiktens skull.
english: We took the detour for the view.

target: Jag vet att hon bor i Malmö.
english: I know that she lives in Malmö.
grammar: "Att" introduces a that-clause. Word order in subordinate clause: subject + inte + verb.

target: Hon sa att det var kallt ute.
english: She said that it was cold outside.
grammar: In att-clauses, word order changes: "inte" comes BEFORE the verb: "att det inte var kallt."

target: Vi hoppas att vädret blir bra.
english: We hope that the weather will be nice.
grammar: "Hoppas" is a deponent verb (s-form but active meaning). It takes an att-clause.

target: Han tror att vi kommer i tid.
english: He thinks that we'll be on time.
grammar: "Tro" (believe/think) vs. "tänka" (think/ponder). "Tro" for opinions, "tänka" for mental activity.

target: Jag vet inte om han kommer.
english: I don't know if he's coming.
grammar: "Om" = if/whether in subordinate clauses. Different from "om" = about.

target: Vi undrar om det finns plats.
grammar: "Om" introduces indirect yes/no questions: 'We wonder if/whether there's room.'

english: Tell me if you need help.
grammar: "Om" here means 'if' — subordinate clause word order: subject before verb.

target: Jag frågar om hon vill följa med.
english: I ask if she wants to come along.
grammar: "Fråga om" = ask whether. "Följa med" = come along — very common particle verb.

target: När jag var liten bodde vi i Kiruna.
english: When I was little we lived in Kiruna.
grammar: "När" = when. Subordinate clause, so "inte" goes BEFORE the verb inside it.

target: Jag ringer dig när jag kommer.
english: I'll call you when I arrive.
grammar: "När" + present tense for future events in Swedish: "when I come" not "when I will come."

target: Vi äter när maten är klar.
english: We eat when the food is ready.
grammar: "Klar" = ready/done. Subordinate clause keeps normal word order after "när."

target: Sedan hon flyttade hit har hon trivts.
english: Since she moved here she has enjoyed it.
grammar: "Sedan" = since. "Trivts" = enjoyed being here (deponent verb "trivas").

target: Innan vi åker måste vi handla.
english: Before we leave we must shop.
grammar: "Innan" = before (time). Subordinate clause follows.

english: We waited until the rain stopped.

target: Medan vi åt berättade hon historien.
english: While we ate she told the story.
grammar: "Medan" = while. Used for simultaneous actions.

target: Jag kan inte sova eftersom det är ljust ute.
english: I can't sleep because it's light outside.
grammar: "Eftersom" = because/since. Triggers subordinate word order.

target: Trots att det regnade gick vi ut.
english: Although it rained we went out.
grammar: "Trots att" = although/despite that.

target: Vi stannade hemma för att det snöade.
english: We stayed home because it was snowing.
grammar: "För att" = because (reason). Not to be confused with "för att" = in order to (purpose).

target: Så snart jag kommer hem ringer jag dig.
english: As soon as I get home I'll call you.

target: Innan du går, kan du stänga fönstret?
english: Before you go, can you close the window?

target: Jag tror att det blir en fin dag.
english: I think it will be a nice day.

target: Hon berättade att hon hade fått jobbet.
english: She told us she had gotten the job.
grammar: Reported speech uses past perfect (hade fått) when the original was past tense (fick).

target: Vi är glada att ni kunde komma.
english: We are happy that you could come.

target: Jag vet att du har rätt.
english: I know that you are right.

target: Om det regnar stannar vi inne.
english: If it rains we'll stay inside.
grammar: "Om" = if (conditional). The subordinate clause often comes first.

target: Om vi skyndar oss hinner vi.
english: If we hurry we'll make it.

target: Jag skulle bli glad om du hjälpte mig.
english: I would be happy if you helped me.

target: Även om det regnar går vi ut.
english: Even if it rains we'll go out.

target: Så fort tåget stannar kliver vi av.
english: As soon as the train stops we get off.

target: Förrän solen gick ner satt vi ute.
english: Until the sun went down we sat outside.

target: Varje gång jag besöker dem bjuds jag på fika.
english: Every time I visit them I'm offered fika.

target: Hon jobbar extra för att kunna resa.
english: She works extra to be able to travel.

target: Vi sparar pengar så att vi kan köpa hus.
english: We save money so that we can buy a house.

target: Jag undrar om det lönar sig.
english: I wonder if it's worth it.

target: Han frågade om jag ville följa med.
english: He asked if I wanted to come along.

target: Vi vet inte om butiken är öppen.
english: We don't know if the store is open.

target: Det beror på om vädret tillåter.
english: It depends on whether the weather permits.

target: Jag hörde att du har fått nytt jobb.
english: I heard that you got a new job.

target: Hon märkte att han var ledsen.
english: She noticed that he was sad.

target: Vi förstod att det var svårt.
english: We understood that it was difficult.

target: Han insåg att han hade fel.
english: He realized that he was wrong.

target: Jag önskar att jag kunde stanna längre.
english: I wish I could stay longer.

target: Problemet är att vi inte har tid.
english: The problem is that we don't have time.

target: Anledningen är att tåget var försenat.
english: The reason is that the train was delayed.

target: Det verkar som att det blir regn.
english: It seems like it will rain.

target: Jag är glad att du mår bra.
english: I'm glad that you feel well.

target: Vi är oroliga att det inte räcker.
english: We're worried that it won't be enough.

target: Hon är säker på att hon har rätt.
english: She is sure that she is right.

target: Det är synd att du inte kan komma.
english: It's a pity that you can't come.

target: Det är bra att du berättade.
Det är viktigt att vi är i tid.
english: It's important that we are on time.

target: Det är möjligt att han har glömt.
english: It's possible that he has forgotten.

target: Det händer att bussen är sen.
english: It happens that the bus is late.

target: Jag minns att vi hade roligt.
english: I remember that we had fun.

target: Vi lovar att vi ska hjälpa.
english: We promise that we will help.

target: Han bestämde att han skulle sluta.
english: He decided that he would quit.

target: Jag hoppas att vi ses igen.
english: I hope that we see each other again.

target: När jag vaknade var det redan ljust.
english: When I woke up it was already light.

target: Medan han jobbade lagade hon mat.
Eftersom det var sent gick vi hem.
english: Since it was late we went home.
grammar: "Eftersom" = since/because. Formal reason clause.

target: Trots att vi var trötta fortsatte vi.
english: Although we were tired we continued.

target: Fastän det var dyrt köpte vi det.
english: Even though it was expensive we bought it.
grammar: "Fastän" = even though/although. Synonym with "trots att."

target: Vi stannade tills det slutade regna.
english: We stayed until it stopped raining.

target: Så länge det är ljust kan vi stanna.
english: As long as it's light we can stay.

target: Ända sedan jag kom hit har jag trivts.
english: Ever since I came here I've enjoyed it.

target: Det var trevligt medan det varade.
english: It was nice while it lasted.

target: Nu när du är här kan vi börja.
english: Now that you're here we can start.

target: Jag berättade för honom att vi inte kunde.
english: I told him that we couldn't.

target: Hon lovade att hon inte skulle berätta.
english: She promised that she wouldn't tell.

target: Vi insisterade på att han inte behövde.
english: We insisted that he didn't need to.

target: Han medgav att han inte hade rätt.
english: He admitted that he wasn't right.

target: Jag föreslog att vi inte skulle vänta.
english: I suggested that we shouldn't wait.

target: Det visar sig att de inte visste.
english: It turns out that they didn't know.

target: Man aldrig vet om det kan hända.
english: You never know if it can happen.

target: Jag undrar när hon kommer tillbaka.
english: I wonder when she'll come back.

target: Vi diskuterade vad vi borde göra.
english: We discussed what we should do.

target: Hon berättade var hon hade bott.
english: She told where she had lived.

target: Jag förstår varför du är upprörd.
Vi vet inte hur lång tid det tar.
english: We don't know how long it takes.

target: Han undrade vem som hade ringt.
Jag frågade vilken väg vi skulle ta.
english: I asked which way we should take.

target: Vi fick reda på att skolan var stängd.
english: We found out that the school was closed.
It became clear that nobody knew.

target: Jag är medveten om att det tar tid.
english: I'm aware that it takes time.

target: Vi utgår från att allt går som planerat.
english: We assume that everything goes as planned.

target: Jag tvivlar på att det fungerar.
Vi räknar med att vara framme klockan fem.
english: We expect to arrive at five o'clock.

target: Det innebär att vi måste ändra planen.
english: It means that we have to change the plan.

target: Hon påpekade att vi hade glömt det.
english: She pointed out that we had forgotten it.

target: Vi antog att det var korrekt.
english: We assumed that it was correct.

target: Det framkom att de inte hade informerats.
english: It emerged that they had not been informed.

grammar: Imperative = verb stem. Group 1: drop -a (prata → prat!). Group 2/3: use stem (kom!).
Single-word imperatives are very common: "Titta! Lyssna! Vänta! Kom!"
"Lite" softens the command: "vänta lite" = wait a moment (not as urgent as just "vänta!").
"Sitta" → imperative "sitt." Irregular strong verb. "Ner" = down.
"Stå" → imperative "stå." "Upp" = up. "Stå upp" = stand up / get up.
Group 1 imperatives keep the -a: öppna! stänga! → stäng! (Group 2, drop -a).
"Stänga" → imperative "stäng." Group 2 verbs drop -a in imperative.
"Vara" imperative is "var" — irregular. "Var tyst!" "Var snäll!"
"Lyssna" → imperative "lyssna" (Group 1 keeps -a). "Lyssna på" = listen to.
"Äta" imperative is "ät" — the bare stem. "Upp" adds completion.
Negative imperative: verb + inte. "Spring inte!" "Gå inte!"
"Var snäll och" + imperative is a polite way to make a request.
"Vara" imperative = "var." Irregular. "Försiktig" = careful.
"Ta det lugnt" = take it easy. "Lugnt" (neuter) for the impersonal "det."
"Ge" imperative is just "ge" — already the stem form.
Group 1 imperative: keep -a. "Tvätta!" "Lyssna!" "Titta!"
"Tänderna" = the teeth. "Tand" → "tänder" (plural) → "tänderna" (def. plural).
"Vakna" imperative keeps the -a: Vakna! But "kom" (Group 4) is just the stem.
Group 1 imperative: keep the -a. "Packa! Stanna! Öppna!"
Particle verbs in imperative: verb + particle. "Stäng av!" "Slå på!"
Taking shoes off indoors is an unspoken rule in Swedish homes.
"Försöka" imperative: "Försök!" Group 2 drops the -a.
"Njuta av" = enjoy. Imperative of Group 4: just the stem "njut."
"Ha" imperative is "ha" — already short. "Ha det bra!" "Ha kul!"
Skansen is the world's first open-air museum, located in Stockholm.
Candles are central to Swedish hygge/mys — Swedes light more candles per capita than anyone.
Reflexive imperatives: sätt dig (sit down), skynda dig (hurry up). "Dig" for 'yourself.'

english: Eat as much as you want!

grammar: "Ta för sig" = help yourself to food. Common at Swedish gatherings.
Double definite: adjective + definite article (den/det/de) AND definite suffix (-en/-et/-na).
"Liten" in definite → "lilla": "det lilla barnet," "den lilla katten."
"Bok" → "böcker" (irregular plural) → "böckerna" (def. plural). "Nya" — all adjectives end -a in definite.

target: Den röda stugan ligger vid sjön.
english: The red cottage is by the lake.
grammar: Röda stugor (red cottages) are the most iconic image of the Swedish countryside.
Double definite: det + gamla + slottet. Three markers of definiteness simultaneously!
Simple definite: just the suffix. "Bil" → "bilen." No adjective = no double definite needed.
Simple definite: "Huset" = the house. Suffix -et for ett-words.

english: The books are on the table.
grammar: Definite plural: -na/-erna. "Böcker" → "böckerna."

target: Den svarta katten sitter i fönstret.
english: The black cat sits in the window.
grammar: Double definite with en-word: den + svarta + katten. Adjective always ends in -a in definite.

target: Det vita huset är till salu.
english: The white house is for sale.
grammar: "Till salu" = for sale. "Vit" → "vita" in definite: den vita, det vita, de vita.
"Dofta" = smell (emit scent). "Gott" (neuter) here because it's an adverb, not adjective.

english: The nice restaurant is fully booked.
grammar: "Fullbokad" = fully booked. Compound: full + bokad. Very common in Swedish.
"Vatten" is ett-word: vattnet (def.). Double definite: det + kalla + vattnet.
"Kort" → "korta" in plural/definite. "Svensk" → "svenska" — same pattern for all adjectives.

target: Den höga byggnaden är ett hotell.
english: The tall building is a hotel.
The old church is from the 1200s.

target: Det mörka molnet ser hotfullt ut.
De långa nätterna är typiska för vintern.
english: The long nights are typical for winter.

target: Mannen i hörnet läser en bok.
english: The man in the corner reads a book.
grammar: When there's no adjective, no double definite: just "mannen," not "den mannen."

english: The girl with the red hair.
grammar: "Hår" is ett-word: "det röda håret" — double definite with adjective.
Simple definite (no adjective) = just the suffix: "sol" → "solen."

english: The lake glitters in the sun.
grammar: Both "sjön" and "solen" are simple definite — no double definite needed.

english: The mountain is visible far away.

grammar: "Himmel" → "himlen" in definite. The -el drops the e.

english: The book I'm reading is exciting.
grammar: When a relative clause follows, no double definite: "boken jag läser."

english: The movie we saw was good.
The food that is served is good.
The music being played is classical.
The people at the square celebrate.

target: Den gula bussen kör till centrum.
english: The yellow bus goes to the center.
The warm buns are freshly made.
The white snow covers the ground.

target: De smala gatorna i Gamla Stan.
english: The narrow streets in the Old Town.

target: Det stora fönstret ger mycket ljus.
english: The big window gives a lot of light.

target: De moderna byggnaderna i Hammarby Sjöstad.
english: The modern buildings in Hammarby Sjöstad.
The popular restaurant on the corner.

target: Det kända museet mitt i stan.
english: The famous museum in the middle of town.

grammar: In definite singular with adjective, -e ending is used for male persons: "den duktige läraren."
Female/non-gendered definite adjective uses -a: "den duktiga läkaren."

english: The other countries in the Nordics.
The night is short in summer.
Spring comes late in the north.

target: Den svenska flaggan är blå och gul.
english: The Swedish flag is blue and yellow.
The Swedish system is well known.

grammar: Swedish education is free from preschool through university.

english: The calm life in the countryside.

grammar: Sweden is one of the top coffee-consuming nations in the world.

english: The great interest in outdoor life.
The open landscape in the south.
The still lake by the cabin.
The mighty mountains in the west.
The thousands of islands in the archipelago.
The Swedish right to roam is unique.
grammar: Allemansrätten allows everyone to walk, camp, and pick berries on any land.

english: The traditional fika at three o'clock.

target: De stora företagen som Volvo och IKEA.
english: The big companies like Volvo and IKEA.

grammar: "Smörgåsbord" is one of few Swedish words used in English.

target: Mannen som bor här är min granne.
english: The man who lives here is my neighbor.
grammar: "Som" is the all-purpose relative pronoun — it replaces who, which, that.

target: Boken som jag läser är spännande.
english: The book that I'm reading is exciting.
grammar: "Som" can be omitted when it's the object: "Boken jag läser" is also correct.

target: Huset som ligger vid sjön är gammalt.
english: The house that is by the lake is old.
grammar: "Som" as subject cannot be omitted: always keep it.

target: Flickan som sjunger har en fin röst.
english: The girl who sings has a beautiful voice.
grammar: "Som" is the universal relative pronoun in spoken Swedish — works for who, which, that.

target: Filmen som vi såg igår var bra.
english: The movie that we saw yesterday was good.
grammar: Past tense in relative clause: "som vi såg" (that we saw).

target: Katten som sitter i fönstret är min.
english: The cat that sits in the window is mine.
grammar: In relative clauses, "som" is always the subject or object. Never omit it like English 'that.'

target: Restaurangen som ligger på hörnet är ny.
english: The restaurant on the corner is new.
grammar: "Som ligger" = that is located. Very common pattern.

target: Människorna som bor här är vänliga.
english: The people who live here are friendly.
grammar: "Människor" → "människorna" (the people). Double definite: article + noun suffix.

target: Tåget som avgår klockan nio är snabbt.
english: The train that departs at nine is fast.
grammar: Relative clauses go right after the noun they describe, just like in English.

target: Läraren som undervisar oss är duktig.
english: The teacher who teaches us is skilled.
grammar: "Duktig" means good/clever at something. Not to be confused with "snäll" (kind).

target: Barnet som leker i parken är glatt.
english: The child playing in the park is happy.
grammar: "Som" never changes form — same for subjects, objects, en/ett/plural. Universal relative pronoun.

target: Vägen som leder till sjön är lång.
english: The road that leads to the lake is long.
grammar: "Leda" = lead. "Sjön" = the lake (en sjö). In relative clauses, verb order stays normal.

target: Staden som vi besökte var vacker.
english: The city that we visited was beautiful.
grammar: "Som" as object — in formal Swedish you can use "vilken/vilket/vilka" instead, but "som" is standard.

english: The music being played is nice.
grammar: "Som spelas" = that is being played. S-passive in relative clause — very natural in Swedish.

target: Kaffet som serveras här är gott.
english: The coffee served here is good.
grammar: "Serveras" = is served (s-passive). "Gott" = good/tasty (neuter, matching "kaffet").

target: Kvinnan som jobbar på banken är trevlig.
english: The woman who works at the bank is nice.
grammar: After "som" as subject, the verb agrees with the antecedent: "kvinnan som jobbar" (she works).

target: Blomman som växer där är sällsynt.
english: The flower that grows there is rare.
grammar: "Sällsynt" = rare. "Växa" = grow (intransitive). Group 2: växer, växte, växt.

target: Hunden som springer i parken tillhör oss.
english: The dog running in the park belongs to us.
grammar: "Tillhöra" = belong to. Formal. Everyday: "är vår" (is ours).

target: Problemet som vi har är allvarligt.
english: The problem we have is serious.

target: Personen som ringde lämnade inget meddelande.
english: The person who called left no message.

target: Bussen som stannar här går till centrum.
english: The bus that stops here goes to the center.

target: Maten som lagas hemma smakar bäst.
english: Food that is cooked at home tastes best.

target: Stolen som står i hörnet är trasig.
english: The chair in the corner is broken.

target: Cykeln som är parkerad utanför är ny.
english: The bicycle parked outside is new.

target: Grannarna som bor ovanför är tysta.
english: The neighbors who live upstairs are quiet.

target: Brevet som kom idag var viktigt.
english: The letter that came today was important.

target: Barnen som leker ute har roligt.
english: The children playing outside are having fun.

target: Skolan som ligger här är bra.
english: The school that is located here is good.

target: Klockan som hänger på väggen är gammal.
english: The clock hanging on the wall is old.

target: Parken som vi besöker varje dag.
english: The park that we visit every day.

target: Jobbet som hon söker är i Malmö.
english: The job she is applying for is in Malmö.

target: Kursen som jag går på är intressant.
english: The course I'm taking is interesting.

target: Vännen som jag träffade igår mår bra.
english: The friend I met yesterday is doing well.

target: Platsen som vi hittade var perfekt.
english: The place we found was perfect.

target: Hotellet som vi bor på ligger centralt.
english: The hotel we stay at is centrally located.

target: Svaret som hon gav var oväntat.
english: The answer she gave was unexpected.

target: Idén som han föreslog var bra.
english: The idea he suggested was good.

target: Klänningen som hon har på sig är fin.
english: The dress she is wearing is nice.

target: Bilen som parkerar där tillhör grannen.
english: The car parking there belongs to the neighbor.

target: Fågeln som sjunger i trädet är en koltrast.
english: The bird singing in the tree is a blackbird.

target: Sjön som vi badar i är varm.
english: The lake we swim in is warm.

target: Väskan som jag köpte var dyr.
english: The bag I bought was expensive.

target: Sången som spelas på radion är populär.
english: The song playing on the radio is popular.

target: Dagen som vi valt passar bra.
english: The day we chose suits well.

target: Allt som glittrar är inte guld.
english: All that glitters is not gold.

target: Något som jag aldrig har sett.
Den som väntar på något gott.
english: He who waits for something good.
grammar: "Den som väntar på något gott väntar aldrig för länge" — a Swedish proverb.

target: Det var det bästa som hänt mig.
english: It was the best thing that happened to me.

target: Allt som vi behöver finns här.

english: He who asks gets an answer.

target: Det som hände igår var oväntat.
Det här är boken som jag pratade om.
english: This is the book I talked about.
grammar: Prepositions stay at the end: "boken som jag pratade OM" not "om som jag pratade."

target: Stolen som vi sitter på är bekväm.
english: The chair we're sitting on is comfortable.
The question we discussed for a long time.

target: Orten som vi åkte till var tyst.
english: The town we went to was quiet.

target: Skolan som barnen går i är nyrenoverad.
english: The school the children go to is newly renovated.

target: Rummet som vi sitter i är ljust.
english: The room we're sitting in is bright.

target: Företaget som jag jobbar på expanderar.
english: The company I work at is expanding.

target: Staden som hon kommer ifrån är liten.
english: The city she comes from is small.
The project we are working on.

target: Huset som vi tittar på är dyrt.
english: The house we're looking at is expensive.

target: Kursen som jag anmälde mig till.
english: The course I signed up for.

target: Receptet som jag fick av henne.
english: The recipe I got from her.
The road that leads through the forest.
The river that flows through the city.
The path that goes through the valley.
The bridge that connects the islands.
The forest that stretches for miles.
The mountains visible on the horizon.
The beach we used to visit.

target: Stugan som familjen ägt i generationer.
english: The cabin the family has owned for generations.
The church that was built in the Middle Ages.
The museum that shows Viking history.

target: Seden som är typisk för midsommar.

english: The food served at the Christmas table.

target: Dansen som man dansar runt stången.
english: The dance you dance around the pole.

target: Allt som hör till svensk kultur.
english: Everything that belongs to Swedish culture.

target: Känslan som man inte kan beskriva.
english: The feeling that you can't describe.
The moment that is never forgotten.
The decision that shaped the future.

target: Resan som vi minns för alltid.

english: The experience that taught me the most.
The words that were never said.
The book that changed my life.
The door is opened at eight.
grammar: S-passive: add -s to the verb. "Öppna" → "öppnas." Used for general/habitual actions.

english: The food is cooked by the chef.
grammar: S-passive is more common than bli-passive for habitual/general statements.

english: The book was written by Astrid Lindgren.
grammar: Past s-passive: "skriva" → "skrevs." The agent uses "av" (by).
Past s-passive: "byggdes." Used for historical facts and completed events.

english: The door was opened by the wind.
grammar: Bli-passive: "bli" + past participle. Emphasizes a single event or change of state.
"Bli-passive" (bli + past participle) emphasizes the process/event of something happening.

english: The children are woken at seven.
grammar: S-passive present: verb stem + -s. "Väcka" → "väcks."

english: The window was closed by the cleaner.
grammar: "Av" introduces the agent in passive: stängdes av = was closed by. Often omitted.
S-passive (serveras) for habitual/general actions. Bli-passive for specific events.
Past s-passive: skicka → skickades. Add -s to past tense: skickade + s.
Modal + s-passive infinitive: "kan köpas," "måste göras," "bör läsas."
"Lösa" → "löstes" (was solved). S-passive past of Group 2 verbs: -te → -tes.

english: The news was reported in the newspaper.
grammar: "Rapporteras" (present), "rapporterades" (past). S-passive is very common in Swedish news.

english: The law was passed by parliament.
grammar: "Riksdagen" = the Swedish parliament. "Antog" → "antogs" — just add -s for s-passive past.
"Bli-passive" + past participle emphasizes the event/change: "blev försenat" = got delayed.

target: Boken har översatts till tjugo språk.
english: The book has been translated into twenty languages.
grammar: "Har översatts" = has been translated. Perfect s-passive: har + supine + s.

english: The house is going to be sold.
grammar: "Ska säljas" = will be sold. Modal + infinitive s-passive. Very common in real estate.

english: The street is being repaired right now.
grammar: "Just nu" = right now. S-passive for ongoing actions: "repareras" = is being repaired.

english: The movie was shown in the cinema.
grammar: "Visas" (present) → "visades" (past). "På bio" = at the cinema (no article needed).
"Tvätta" → "tvättades" (past s-passive). Group 1 verbs: -ades ending in past passive.

english: The result was published this morning.
grammar: "Publicera" → "publicerades" (past s-passive). "I morse" = this morning.
"Ställa in" = cancel. S-passive past: "ställdes in." The particle stays.

english: The museum is visited by thousands of tourists.
grammar: "Tusentals" = thousands of. "Besöks av" = is visited by. S-passive present.

english: The car was repaired by the mechanic.
grammar: "Reparera" → "reparerades." "-ades" is Group 1 past s-passive ending.

english: The food is prepared with local ingredients.
The book was printed in a thousand copies.
The price was decided by the board.
The church was restored last year.
The City Hall was inaugurated in 1923.
The Nobel Prize is awarded in December.
grammar: The Nobel Prize ceremony takes place at Stockholm Concert Hall on December 10th.

target: Festen blev inställd på grund av regn.
english: The party was cancelled because of rain.
The contract was signed by both parties.

target: Rapporten ska lämnas in på fredag.
english: The report is to be submitted on Friday.
The food should be eaten warm.
The ticket can be booked in the app.
The book should be read by everyone.
The work is performed by a team.
The lawn is mowed every week.
The garbage is picked up on Tuesdays.
The mail is delivered at ten.
The laundry is hung outside in summer.
The dishes are done after dinner.
The floor is mopped every day.
The bed is made in the morning.
The flowers are watered every other day.
The children are picked up at four.
Dinner is prepared by the whole family.
The coffee is brewed at about eight.
The presents are opened on Christmas Eve.
grammar: In Sweden, presents are opened on Christmas Eve (the 24th), not Christmas Day.

english: The Lucia procession is seen in December.

target: Sillen läggs in i god tid.
english: The herring is pickled well in advance.
The rules are followed by everyone.
The budget was set in January.
The annual report was presented at the meeting.
The project was completed on time.
The agreement was extended by one year.
The salary was raised by three percent.
The position was filled in March.
The course is given every fall.
The test was graded by the teacher.
The application was submitted on time.
The answer was received with joy.
The invitation was accepted by everyone.
The match was played in rain.
The award was given at the gala.
The bridge was built over two years.
The island was explored by the researchers.
The tradition is preserved by the local population.
The language is spoken by ten million.
The party was organized by the association.
The song was played on the radio.
The information was shared with everyone.
The medal was awarded to the winner.
The verdict was pronounced in court.
The matter was handled at the meeting.
The document was approved by the boss.
The house was painted in Falu red paint.
grammar: Falu rödfärg is the iconic red paint from Falun copper mine, used since the 1600s.

english: The cabin was inherited by the children.
The law was enacted in the 1800s.
The find was reported to the police.
The road was widened last summer.
The decision has already been made.
The place has not been decided yet.

grammar: Deponent verbs end in -s but are not passive. "Hoppas" = hope, "minnas" = remember.

english: I remember the summer in Dalarna.
